12:0.rendering: Keep on loving each other as brothers and sisters.
12:0.note: The Greek word for "brotherly love" here is philadelphia, an ancient virtue now redirected toward the new family Jesus creates.

12:1.rendering: Do not neglect to show hospitality to strangers, for by this some have entertained angels without knowing it.
12:1.note: The Greek word for "entertained" (xenizo) plays on "strangers" (xenos), echoing Abraham's surprising visitors in Genesis 18.

12:2.rendering: Remember those in prison, as though you were in prison with them, and those who are mistreated, since you yourselves are also in the body.
12:2.note: The phrase "as though bound with them" echoes the ancient idea of solidarity in the body, linking back to the earlier image of the church as a single body in Hebrews 10:33 and 12:1.

12:3.rendering: Marriage should be honored by all, and the marriage bed kept undefiled, for God will judge the sexually immoral and adulterers.
12:3.note: The Greek word for "undefiled" (amiantos) echoes the purity required for temple worship, now applied to the marriage covenant.

12:4.rendering: Keep your life free from the love of money. Be content with what you have, for he has said, "I will never leave you or forsake you."
12:4.note: The promise "I will never leave you or forsake you" echoes God's words to Israel in Deuteronomy 31:6, now spoken to the whole community facing pressure.

12:5.rendering: So we can say with confidence, "The Lord is my helper, I will not be afraid. What can a mere human do to me?"
12:5.note: This bold confidence echoes Psalm 118:6, turning an ancient temple song of rescue into a word for scattered believers facing pressure.

12:6.rendering: Remember your leaders, who spoke God's word to you. Consider the outcome of their way of life and imitate their faith.
12:6.note: The Greek word for "outcome" (ekbasis) means both the end result of their lives and the way they exited this world, inviting us to trace how their faith shaped their entire story.

12:7.rendering: Jesus the Messiah is the same yesterday, today, and forever.
12:7.note: This echoes God's self-revelation to Moses as "I AM" in Exodus 3:14, anchoring the letter's call to endurance in the unchanging character of Israel's God now revealed in Jesus.

12:8.rendering: Do not be carried away by various strange teachings. For it is good for the heart to be strengthened by grace, not by foods, since those who eat them have not benefited.
12:8.note: The Greek word for "carried away" echoes the wilderness wanderings, warning against drifting after novel teachings instead of the stable grace that truly nourishes the heart.

12:9.rendering: We have an altar from which those who serve in the tabernacle have no right to eat.
12:9.note: This echoes the Day of Atonement ritual in Leviticus 16, where the sin offering's meat was burned outside the camp, not eaten by the priests.

12:10.rendering: For the bodies of those animals whose blood is brought into the holy place by the high priest as an offering for sin are burned outside the camp.
12:10.note: The phrase "outside the camp" echoes the Day of Atonement ritual in Leviticus 16:27, marking a place of curse and rejection that Hebrews will soon tie to Jesus' own death.

12:11.rendering: Therefore Jesus also suffered outside the city gate in order to make the people holy through his own blood.
12:11.note: The Greek word for "make holy" echoes the sanctuary rituals where blood outside the camp purified what was inside.

12:12.rendering: So let us go out to him outside the camp, bearing his disgrace.
12:12.note: This echoes Israel's wilderness wanderings and the Day of Atonement scapegoat driven outside the camp, inviting believers to embrace Jesus' shame as their true home.

12:13.rendering: For here we have no lasting city, but we are seeking the one that is coming.
12:13.note: The Greek word for "lasting" echoes the wilderness tabernacle's temporary nature, inviting readers to live as sojourners like Abraham who looked for a city with foundations.

12:14.rendering: Through him, then, let us continually offer up a sacrifice of praise to God, that is, the fruit of lips confessing his name.
12:14.note: The phrase "sacrifice of praise" echoes the Old Testament's todah, a thank offering that was actually a shared meal of celebration after deliverance.

12:15.rendering: And do not neglect doing good and sharing what you have, for with such sacrifices God is well pleased.
12:15.note: The word for "sacrifices" deliberately echoes the temple language of Leviticus, showing that everyday generosity now counts as the acceptable offering.

12:16.rendering: Obey your leaders and submit to them, for they keep watch over your souls as those who will give an account. Let them do this with joy and not with groaning, for that would be of no advantage to you.
12:16.note: The Greek word for "obey" here is peithesthe, better rendered "be persuaded by" your leaders, a call to trust rather than blind compliance.

12:17.rendering: Keep praying for us, for we are confident that we have a clear conscience, since we desire to live honorably in every way.
12:17.note: The rare Greek phrase for "clear conscience" echoes how Paul defends his integrity before governors and kings in Acts, inviting the community to stand with their leaders in shared honor.

12:18.rendering: And I urge you all the more to do this, so that I may be restored to you more quickly.
12:18.note: The Greek word for "restored" (apokatastatho) carries the flavor of being "put back in place," echoing Israel's hope of restoration after exile.

12:19.rendering: Now may the God of peace, who brought up from the dead the great shepherd of the sheep by the blood of the eternal covenant, our Lord Jesus,
12:19.note: The phrase "brought up from the dead" echoes Isaiah 63:11's cry for the shepherd of the flock to be brought up from the sea, now fulfilled in resurrection.

12:20.rendering: equip you with every good thing to do his will, carrying out in us what is pleasing in his sight, through Jesus Christ, to whom be the glory forever and ever. Amen.
12:20.note: The Greek verb "equip" (katartizo) echoes its use in 1 Peter 5:10 and the restoring of broken nets in the Gospels, hinting at God mending us for his purposes.

12:21.rendering: Brothers and sisters, I urge you to bear with this word of exhortation, for I have written to you briefly.
12:21.note: The Greek term for "word of exhortation" is the exact phrase used in Acts 13:15 to describe a synagogue sermon, hinting that Hebrews was crafted as a preached message rather than a typical letter.

12:22.rendering: Our brother Timothy has been released. If he comes soon, I will see you with him.
12:22.note: This casual mention of Timothy's release echoes the prison epistles, showing how the early Christian network wove personal updates into letters meant for communal meditation.

12:23.rendering: Greet all your leaders and all the saints. Those from Italy send you their greetings.
12:23.note: This closing greeting echoes the ancient letter form while revealing a community of Italian believers sending warm wishes home, a subtle nod to the letter's likely Roman audience.

12:24.rendering: Grace be with all of you.
12:24.note: This final word echoes the opening greeting in 1:2, bookending the letter with the same lavish gift that launched creation itself.
